items.q: Is bandwidth the distance a signal can travel?
items.correct: No - bandwidth is the capacity of a connection in bits per second; how far a signal reaches is range, a separate property.
items.misconception: Yes - higher bandwidth means the Wi-Fi reaches further.
items.random_wrong: Yes - bandwidth is measured in metres.
items.why: The word 'band' suggests physical reach. Keep the units straight: capacity is bits per second, range is metres - upgrading one does not upgrade the other.

items.q: In a client-server network, are the clients just dumb screens with no processing power?
items.correct: No - clients are full computers that run their own software; they simply request central services such as file storage, email or printing from servers.
items.misconception: Yes - only the server does any computing in a client-server network.
items.random_wrong: Yes - clients are keyboards connected directly to the server.
items.why: The hierarchy suggests helplessness. The model describes who provides services, not who can compute - a powerful gaming PC is still a client of its file server.

items.correct: No - a LAN is defined by its small geographic area, not its medium; wireless LANs (Wi-Fi) are still LANs.
items.misconception: Yes - wireless networks are never classed as LANs.
items.random_wrong: Yes - LAN stands for Line-Attached Network.
items.why: Diagrams of cabled offices fix the image. The definition is about coverage area and ownership; the W in WLAN just specifies the wireless medium.

items.correct: Forward packets between different networks towards their destination
items.misconception: Print documents for the whole office
items.why: It's Forward packets between different networks towards their destination. “Print documents for the whole office” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Central servers provide services that client devices request
items.misconception: Every device must act as a server
items.random_wrong: No device may use the internet
items.why: It's Central servers provide services that client devices request. “Every device must act as a server” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: One advantage of a peer-to-peer network is that it:
items.correct: Needs no expensive dedicated server, so it is cheap and simple for small setups
items.misconception: Gives the best security for large companies
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Needs no expensive dedicated server, so it is cheap and simple for small setups. “Gives the best security for large companies” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The maximum rate at which data can be transferred over a connection
items.misconception: The physical width of the network cable
items.random_wrong: The number of users on a network
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The maximum rate at which data can be transferred over a connection. “The physical width of the network cable” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A connection has a bandwidth of 100 Mbps. What is the maximum number of megabits it can transfer in 5 seconds?
items.why: Exact answer: 500 Mbit. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
